createdBy: Claude Code - RDX Comprehensive Infotainment TARA Example v1.0

itemDefinition.title: Advanced Infotainment System ECU
itemDefinition.boundary: Complete infotainment ecosystem including head unit, displays, connectivity modules, and integration with vehicle systems for entertainment, navigation, communication, and vehicle control interfaces
itemDefinition.architecture: ARM Cortex-A78 octa-core SoC, Android Automotive OS 14, 8GB RAM, 256GB storage, dedicated GPU, 4G/5G cellular modem, WiFi 6E, Bluetooth 5.3, multiple CAN interfaces, Ethernet backbone, hardware security module (HSM)
itemDefinition.environment: Production luxury SUV, global markets including US, EU, China, 2027 model year launch, ambient temperature -40°C to +85°C, automotive EMC requirements, IP54 ingress protection
assumptions: Hardware security module (HSM) provides secure key storage
Verified boot process ensures system integrity
Android Automotive security framework is properly configured
Network firewalls isolate critical vehicle systems
Regular security updates will be deployed via OTA
Users will connect personal devices and install third-party apps
Physical access to vehicle interior is possible for legitimate users
Emergency services integration requires always-on connectivity

assets.title: Personal User Data and Privacy Information

damageScenarios.title: Severe privacy violation through unauthorized data access
damageScenarios.description: Comprehensive breach of user privacy including location tracking, personal communications, media consumption patterns, and behavioral data leading to surveillance, discrimination, and personal safety risks

damageScenarios.title: Critical safety hazard via vehicle control manipulation
damageScenarios.description: Unauthorized manipulation of safety-critical vehicle functions including braking, steering, acceleration, or warning systems leading to potential accidents, injuries, or fatalities
references: NHTSA Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standards

damageScenarios.title: Financial fraud through compromised payment systems
damageScenarios.description: Unauthorized access to payment information, subscription services, or in-vehicle commerce leading to fraudulent transactions, identity theft, and financial losses
references: PCI DSS Payment Card Industry Standards

damageScenarios.title: Identity theft through comprehensive data aggregation
damageScenarios.description: Systematic collection of personal identifiers, behavioral patterns, financial information, and biometric data enabling sophisticated identity theft and impersonation attacks
references: FTC Identity Theft Red Flags Rule

damageScenarios.title: Vehicle immobilization or operational disruption
damageScenarios.description: Complete or partial loss of vehicle functionality through system compromise, rendering the vehicle inoperable or unsafe to drive, potentially stranding occupants in dangerous situations

damageScenarios.title: Stalking and harassment enabled by location tracking
damageScenarios.description: Malicious use of location data and personal information to facilitate stalking, harassment, domestic violence, or other targeted harmful activities against vehicle users

damageScenarios.title: Copyright infringement through unauthorized content access
damageScenarios.description: Unauthorized access, copying, or distribution of copyrighted media content, leading to legal liabilities and intellectual property violations

damageScenarios.title: Comprehensive security breach with system-wide compromise
damageScenarios.description: Large-scale compromise of cryptographic systems enabling persistent unauthorized access, data manipulation, and control over multiple vehicle functions and user accounts

threatScenarios.title: Malicious application exploitation
threatScenarios.cause: Installation of malicious third-party application with excessive permissions or exploitation of legitimate app vulnerabilities to access sensitive user data

threatScenarios.title: Network traffic interception and manipulation
threatScenarios.cause: Man-in-the-middle attacks on cellular, WiFi, or Bluetooth communications to intercept, modify, or inject malicious data and commands

threatScenarios.title: Unauthorized physical access exploitation
threatScenarios.cause: Physical access to vehicle interior or service interfaces to extract cryptographic keys, install malicious hardware, or gain unauthorized system access

threatScenarios.title: Supply chain compromise
threatScenarios.cause: Compromise of hardware or software components during manufacturing, distribution, or update processes introducing backdoors or malicious functionality

threatScenarios.title: Malicious insider threat
threatScenarios.cause: Authorized personnel (developers, service technicians, or cloud service employees) abusing legitimate access to steal data or insert malicious code

threatScenarios.title: Cloud service infrastructure compromise
threatScenarios.cause: Compromise of cloud infrastructure hosting user data, navigation services, or update servers enabling large-scale data theft or service disruption

threatScenarios.title: Emergency services system exploitation
threatScenarios.cause: Exploitation of emergency call (eCall) system to track vehicles, disrupt emergency response, or gain unauthorized access to vehicle systems

threatScenarios.title: Software update hijacking
threatScenarios.cause: Interception or substitution of legitimate software updates with malicious versions to gain persistent system access or install backdoors

attackPaths.title: Malicious app installation → Data exfiltration
steps: Develop malicious app mimicking legitimate functionality
Upload to official or sideloaded app store
Social engineer user to install app
Request excessive permissions during installation
Access sensitive user data and location information
Exfiltrate data to attacker-controlled servers

attackPaths.title: WiFi man-in-the-middle → Payment interception
steps: Set up malicious WiFi hotspot with legitimate-sounding name
Wait for vehicle to connect to hotspot
Intercept all network traffic including HTTPS
Use SSL/TLS downgrade attacks or certificate spoofing
Capture payment credentials during in-vehicle purchases
Clone payment information for fraudulent use

attackPaths.title: Physical USB access → Key extraction
steps: Gain physical access to vehicle interior
Connect malicious USB device to infotainment system
Exploit USB autorun or driver vulnerabilities
Escalate privileges to root/system level
Extract cryptographic keys from hardware security module
Use keys to decrypt user data or forge authentication

attackPaths.title: Supply chain firmware compromise → Backdoor installation
steps: Compromise software development environment
Insert malicious code into firmware update
Sign malicious firmware with legitimate certificates
Distribute through official update channels
Activate backdoor after installation
Maintain persistent access for data theft
references: Software Supply Chain Attack Case Studies

attackPaths.title: Service technician data theft
steps: Malicious service technician gains authorized access
Connect diagnostic equipment during routine service
Use legitimate credentials to access customer data
Extract personal information and usage patterns
Copy cryptographic keys and authentication tokens
Sell data on black market or use for identity theft

attackPaths.title: Cloud database breach → Mass data theft
steps: Identify vulnerable cloud service hosting user data
Exploit unpatched vulnerabilities or misconfigurations
Gain unauthorized access to customer database
Extract personal data from multiple vehicle owners
Access location history and behavioral patterns
Monetize data through sale or targeted attacks

attackPaths.title: Emergency call system spoofing → Vehicle tracking
steps: Intercept or spoof emergency service communications
Trigger false emergency calls to track vehicle locations
Exploit emergency system privileges for system access
Monitor real-time location and occupancy data
Disrupt legitimate emergency response capabilities
Use tracking data for stalking or criminal activities

attackPaths.title: Update server redirection → Malicious firmware
steps: Compromise DNS or network infrastructure
Redirect update requests to attacker-controlled server
Serve malicious firmware disguised as legitimate update
Bypass signature verification through vulnerability
Install backdoor with system-level privileges
Maintain persistent access across system reboots

attackPaths.title: Bluetooth vulnerability exploitation
steps: Discover vehicle Bluetooth services through scanning
Exploit known Bluetooth stack vulnerabilities
Gain unauthorized access to infotainment system
Escalate privileges through local exploits
Access stored user data and system functions
Install persistent malware for ongoing access

attackPaths.title: Voice command injection → Vehicle control
steps: Analyze voice recognition system vulnerabilities
Craft malicious audio commands disguised as media
Trigger voice commands through media playback
Bypass voice authentication through spoofing
Execute unauthorized vehicle control commands
Cause safety hazards through unexpected behaviors

attackFeasibilityRatings.rationale: App development requires moderate technical skills but deployment through app stores is straightforward with unlimited time window for social engineering
WiFi attacks are well-documented with available tools, but require physical proximity and limited attack window
Physical access required with specialized hardware knowledge, but attack execution is relatively quick once access is obtained
Requires sophisticated adversary with extensive resources, deep technical knowledge, and long-term planning but potentially affects many vehicles
Insider threats are highly feasible due to legitimate access and authorization, requiring minimal additional expertise
Cloud attacks require significant technical expertise but can be conducted remotely with standard tools over extended periods
Emergency system attacks require deep protocol knowledge and specialized equipment, but potential for significant impact
Update hijacking requires sophisticated infrastructure compromise and cryptographic bypass capabilities
Bluetooth exploits are well-researched with available tools, but require physical proximity and specific vulnerability conditions
Voice injection attacks require specialized knowledge of audio processing and machine learning systems but can be prepared offline

impactRatings.rationale: Severe privacy violations can result in significant legal penalties under GDPR/CCPA, long-term reputational damage, and loss of customer trust
Safety hazards pose extreme risk to human life, massive legal liability, and catastrophic financial losses including recalls and litigation
Financial fraud directly impacts customers and company reputation, with moderate legal and operational consequences
Identity theft has severe long-term consequences for victims and significant legal and financial liability for the organization
Vehicle immobilization can create safety hazards and has severe operational impact with significant repair and customer service costs
Location-enabled stalking poses serious personal safety risks with significant legal liability and privacy violations
Copyright violations have moderate legal consequences but limited operational and financial impact compared to other damage scenarios
Comprehensive security breaches have maximum impact across all categories requiring extensive incident response and system rebuilding

riskValues.rationale: Medium feasibility combined with very high privacy impact results in high overall risk requiring immediate attention
Low-medium feasibility with high financial impact results in medium risk requiring mitigation controls
Medium feasibility with maximum security breach impact results in very high risk requiring comprehensive controls
High feasibility for sophisticated attackers with maximum impact results in very high risk requiring supply chain security program
High feasibility insider threat with severe identity theft impact requires comprehensive insider threat program
Medium feasibility cloud attack with very high privacy impact requires robust cloud security controls
High feasibility emergency system exploit with maximum safety impact requires specialized emergency system security
High feasibility update attacks with maximum security impact requires secure software delivery infrastructure
Low-medium feasibility with high operational impact results in medium risk requiring Bluetooth security hardening
Medium feasibility voice attacks with maximum safety impact requires voice interface security controls

controls.title: Cloud Infrastructure Security Controls
references: Data Encryption at Rest and in Transit

controls.title: Privacy by Design and Data Minimization

riskTreatmentDecisions.justification: High privacy risk requires strong application isolation and privacy controls to protect user data from malicious applications
Medium financial fraud risk requires comprehensive network security to protect payment information during transmission and storage
Very high security breach risk requires physical security measures and integrity verification to protect against unauthorized access
Very high supply chain risk requires comprehensive verification processes and secure update mechanisms to prevent compromise
Very high insider threat risk requires monitoring systems and data minimization to limit potential damage from malicious insiders
High cloud security risk requires robust cloud controls and incident response capabilities to protect user data
Very high safety risk from emergency system exploits requires specialized authentication and authorization controls
Very high update hijacking risk requires multiple layers of verification and supply chain security controls
Medium Bluetooth risk requires security hardening to prevent unauthorized access through wireless interfaces
High voice injection safety risk requires authentication and validation controls to prevent unauthorized voice commands

relationships.justification: Infotainment ECU directly stores and processes personal user data including contacts, messages, and preferences
Infotainment ECU has interfaces to vehicle control systems through CAN bus connections
Infotainment ECU processes payment information for in-vehicle purchases and services
Infotainment ECU contains GPS/GNSS receivers and stores location history for navigation services
Infotainment ECU contains hardware security module storing cryptographic keys and certificates
Infotainment ECU stores and processes personal media content including music, videos, and photos
Malicious applications directly threaten user data confidentiality through unauthorized access and exfiltration
Malicious apps can access location services and historical location data through Android permissions
Network interception attacks directly threaten payment information during online transactions
Physical access enables hardware attacks against cryptographic key storage in HSM
Supply chain compromise can affect all system assets through backdoors in firmware or hardware components
Insider threats have direct authorized access to user data through legitimate service interfaces
Cloud service compromise directly threatens user data stored in backend systems
Emergency system exploitation enables unauthorized access to real-time location data
Update hijacking can compromise cryptographic systems through malicious firmware installation
Malicious application data access directly causes privacy violations through unauthorized data collection
Network interception of payment data directly enables financial fraud through credential theft
Physical access to cryptographic keys enables comprehensive security breach of all system functions
Supply chain compromise introduces systemic vulnerabilities leading to comprehensive security breaches
Insider access to comprehensive user data enables sophisticated identity theft attacks
Cloud data breach directly causes large-scale privacy violations affecting multiple users
Emergency system exploitation enables location tracking for stalking and harassment activities
Malicious updates can disable vehicle systems leading to immobilization or operational failures
Attack path directly implements the malicious application threat scenario
WiFi man-in-the-middle attack path implements the network interception threat scenario
USB-based attack path implements the physical access threat scenario
Firmware compromise attack path implements the supply chain threat scenario
Service technician attack path implements the insider threat scenario
Cloud database attack path implements the cloud infrastructure compromise threat scenario
Emergency call spoofing attack path implements the emergency services exploitation threat scenario
Update redirection attack path implements the software update hijacking threat scenario
Bluetooth exploitation attack path implements the network interception threat scenario
Voice injection attack path implements the malicious application threat scenario through audio-based exploitation
Attack feasibility rating directly assesses the feasibility of the malicious app installation attack path
Impact rating directly assesses the severity and consequences of the privacy violation damage scenario
Application sandboxing significantly reduces the impact of malicious applications by restricting access to system resources
End-to-end encryption prevents unauthorized access to data during network transmission
Physical tamper detection and response mechanisms prevent unauthorized hardware access and manipulation
Supply chain integrity verification reduces the risk of compromised components and software
Insider threat detection and monitoring reduces but cannot eliminate risks from authorized malicious actors
Comprehensive cloud security controls significantly reduce the risk of cloud infrastructure compromise
Strong authentication and authorization for emergency services prevents unauthorized system access
Secure update verification prevents installation of unauthorized or malicious software updates
Bluetooth security hardening reduces the risk of wireless interface exploitation
Voice authentication and validation prevents unauthorized voice command injection attacks
Privacy by design and data minimization practices reduce the exposure and impact of user data breaches
Cybersecurity incident response program provides protection for all assets through rapid threat detection and response
Malicious applications often work in conjunction with network attacks to exfiltrate stolen data
Physical access attacks are often facilitated by insider knowledge of system architecture and security measures
Supply chain compromises often involve hijacking software update mechanisms to distribute malicious code
Emergency system exploits and malicious apps both enable unauthorized location tracking capabilities
